items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.misconception: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Students lack a reliable rule. For sharp keys: last sharp + 1 semitone = tonic (e.g., F sharp means G major). For flat keys: the second-to-last flat IS the tonic (or memorise: 1 flat = F major).

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: Modulation from C major to G major is to the:
items.why: Correct answer: Dominant. “Subdominant” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Relative minor of C major is:
items.why: It's Am. “Cm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Modulation from C major up to G major moves the tonic by how many semitones?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
